coredata.dc:title: Deep learning assisted logic gates for real-time identification of natural tetracycline antibiotics

coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Ping
Chen, Yuhui
Chang, Hongrong
Liu, Tao
Wang, Jian
Lu, Zhiwei
Sun, Mengmeng
Su, Gehong
Wang, Yanying
Wang, Huimin David
Leung, Chunghang
Rao, Hanbing
Wu, Chun
coredata.dc:description: The overuse and misuse of tetracycline (TCs) antibiotics, including tetracycline (TTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DC), and chlortetracycline (CTC), pose a serious threat to human health. However, current rapid sensing platforms for tetracyclines can only quantify the total amount of TCs mixture, lacking real-time identification of individual components. To address this challenge, we integrated a deep learning strategy with fluorescence and colorimetry-based multi-mode logic gates in our self-designed smartphone-integrated toolbox for the real-time identification of natural TCs. Our ratiometric fluorescent probe (CD-Au NCs@ZIF-8) encapsulated carbon dots and Au NCs in ZIF-8 to prevent false negative or positive results. Additionally, our independently developed WeChat app enabled linear quantification of the four natural TCs using the fluorescence channels. The colorimetric channels were also utilized as outputs of logic gates to achieve real-time identification of the four individual natural tetracyclines. We anticipate this strategy could provide a new perspective for effective control of antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Deep learning
Smartphone
Logic gates
Tetracycline antibiotics
Identification

Multi-mode logic gate for tetracycline detection
Optical and visual sensing of TCs
Practical analysis of TCs in milk and river water
Colorimetric sensing mechanism of different TCs

This work is supported by the following grants: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2023NSFSC0618), Two-Way Support Team Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (P202105), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (22104102, 22301201 and 32271352), Tianfu Emei Young Science & Technology Talents from Sichuan Province (NO. 2618).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) ZIF-8 and (B) CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8. SEM images of (C) ZIF-8 and (D) CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8. (F) EDS and (E and G) SEM Mapping of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8.
(A) XRD and (B) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8 and CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8. (C) The XPS full spectrum of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8. The high resolution XPS peaks of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8, Zn 2p (D), O 1 s (E), and Au 4f (F).

(A) Changes in fluorescence intensity of TCs added containing (a) blank, (b) ZIF-8, (c) Zn2+ and (d) 2-methylimidazole, respectively. (B) FT-IR spectra of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 before and after addition of TCs. (C and D) Changes in fluorescence lifetimes of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8, CDs, and Au NCs before and after addition of TCs (TCs used were DC at 50 μM). (E) UV absorption spectra of four TCs (10 μM) mixed with CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (F) Zeta potentials of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 and TCs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 after addition of different concentrations of (A) DC, (B) OTC, (C) CTC, (D) TTC. CIE chromaticity diagram for (F) DC, (G) OTC, (H) CTC, (I) TTC analysis. A linear fit plot between ΔF and (I) DC, (J) OTC, (K) CTC, and (L) TTC.
(A) The 3D printed cassette of “96 Speckles”. (B) Network rack structure diagram of YOLOv8 algorithm.
The interface and operation flow of WeChat App “96 Speckles”. Analysis of the linear relationship after adding DC (A), OTC (B), TTC (C), and CTC (D) based on the APP.

(A) Chemical structure of the four tetracycline analogues including TTC, DC, CTC, and OTC. (B) UV–vis absorbance spectra of TTC, DC, CTC, and OTC and CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 with added TTC, DC, CTC, and OTC. (C) UV–vis absorbance spectra at 550 nm values of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 with different concentrations of TTC, DC, CTC, and OTC added.

Deep learning-based APP to extract colorimetric eigenvalues of different concentrations of (A) CTC, (B) TTC, (C) DC, and (D) OTC with CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8, respectively. (E) Multimode logic gates based on UV analysis, colorimetric changes, and fluorescence channels.

(A) Process for the preparation of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 based on a deep learning-assisted smartphone for the fluorescence detection of TCs mixture. (B) Detection of four tetracycline analogues using UV and VL analysis, colorimetric changes combined with deep learning. (C) Deep learning assisted logic gates for the identification of four tetracycline analogues.

head.ce:title: Deep learning assisted logic gates for real-time identification of natural tetracycline antibiotics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The overuse and misuse of tetracycline (TCs) antibiotics, including tetracycline (TTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DC), and chlortetracycline (CTC), pose a serious threat to human health. However, current rapid sensing platforms for tetracyclines can only quantify the total amount of TCs mixture, lacking real-time identification of individual components. To address this challenge, we integrated a deep learning strategy with fluorescence and colorimetry-based multi-mode logic gates in our self-designed smartphone-integrated toolbox for the real-time identification of natural TCs. Our ratiometric fluorescent probe (CD-Au NCs@ZIF-8) encapsulated carbon dots and Au NCs in ZIF-8 to prevent false negative or positive results. Additionally, our independently developed WeChat app enabled linear quantification of the four natural TCs using the fluorescence channels. The colorimetric channels were also utilized as outputs of logic gates to achieve real-time identification of the four individual natural tetracyclines. We anticipate this strategy could provide a new perspective for effective control of antibiotics.

Deep learning-assisted multi-modal logic gates to identify four tetracycline analogues using an in-house portable device.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs) antibiotics, including tetracycline (TTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DC) and chlortetracycline (CTC), are widely used in disease treatment and animal husbandry (Li et al., 2023; Qi et al., 2023). The contamination from overuse and misuse of TCs threatens human health (Xu et al., 2024; Yue et al., 2024). Conventional identification methods of the four natural tetracycline analogues mainly rely on high performance liquid chromatography (Nilnit, Jeenno, Vichapong, Siriangkhawut, & Ponhong, 2024; Phomai, Vichapong, Grudpan, & Ponhong, 2023), mass spectrometry (Jiang et al., 2020; Ri et al., 2021) and enzyme immunoassay (Gong, Li, Qing, Zhang, & Feng, 2019). These methods involve tedious sample pre-treatment procedures, application of professional equipment, and time-consuming operation process. Recently, visualization-based rapid sensing platforms have demonstrated superior advantages in terms of efficiency, operability, and sensitivity, including fluorescence (Arkin et al., 2023; Jia et al., 2021; L. Zhang et al., 2024), colorimetry (C. Sun et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023), electrochemistry techniques (Chou, Dai, Yuan, & Shen, 2023). However, these platforms can only achieve the quantitative or qualitative detection of their mixture, and cannot facilitate the identification of individual natural tetracycline analogues. Therefore, methods for the sensitive, portable, and real-time identification of the individual tetracyclines are highly desirable for the effective monitoring of the use of antibiotics.

In recent years, a variety of logic gates such as AND, OR, XOR, NOR, INHIBIT, have been manufactured (Deng et al., 2023; Guo, Ren, & Wang, 2022; Li, Chen, Zhao, & Ma, 2023). The logic elements provide digital outputs in the form of “YES” or “NO” to reflect multiple inputs of various analytes (Arkin et al., 2023; Manayil Parambil et al., 2023; L. Yu et al., 2023). This feature poses the critical need in smart biosensing for “rapid screening” (L. Shi et al., 2023). To date, fluorescent(Abbasi-Moayed, Bigdeli, & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2022; H. Zhang et al., 2023), electrochemical (Y. Yu, Guo, Jiang, Zhang, & Cai, 2021), colorimetric (Borah, Kaka, & Tamuly, 2023; Manayil Parambil et al., 2023), and other types of logic gates have been fabricated to allow for simultaneous detection of multiple analytes, and hence increase detection accuracy and streamline analytical processes. Zn-MOF doped with Tb3+ and Cu2+ ions were prepared to construct logic gates for aspartic acid detection with a detection limit at 0.54 ppm (Ji, Zheng, Gao, & Liu, 2019). The inputs of most logic gates usually originate from a single channel. Multi-channel inputs overcome the limitations of single data and allow for more accurate detection of target substances (Majeed et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2021). Therefore, converting logic gates into convenient, economical, and easy-to-operate sensing systems would greatly broaden their applications.

Current rapid sensing platforms for natural tetracyclines can only quantify the total amount of TCs mixture, lacking simultaneous identification of the four analogues in real time. To address this challenge, we integrated a deep learning strategy with fluorescence and colorimetry-based multi-mode logic gates in our self-designed smartphone-integrated toolbox for the real-time identification of the four natural TCs. Previous reports have suggested that the triple-emission fluorescent probe could allow for reduction of false negative or false positive results (Wu et al., 2023). In this study, a triple-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed by encapsulating the fluorescent carbon dots and BSA-capped Au NPs in ZIF-8 to prevent false negative or positive results. To realize the rapid input and output of data, we have designed our own “RGB” and “96 Speckles” applets by combining 96-well plates or cuvettes with smartphones and YOLO algorithms (Scheme 1A-B). The introduction of machine learning minimizes the complexity of data output and its combination with smartphones enables rapid recording (Li et al., 2024; Tang et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2021). The logic gates were designed based on three-inputs including fluorescence (FL), UV and visible light (VL) channels (Hao et al., 2021; Mu, Shi, She, Chang, & Lee, 2009). The high-throughput rapid classification of the four TCs analogues was achieved with the assistance of a smartphone (Scheme 1C).
The details for reagents, material synthesis, tools and equipment are displayed in the Supporting Information .

Synthesis of Au NCs: Au NCs was prepared according to modified literature methods (Fu, Chen, & Jia, 2022). HAuCl4·4H2O and bovine serum protein were used as the precursor and the reducing and stabilizing agent, respectively. A mixed solution of HAuCl4·4H2O (0.02 g/mL, 5 mL) and protein powder solution (50 mg/mL, 5 mL) was pipetted and magnetically stirred for 5 min. Afterwards, a NaOH (1 M) solution was used to bring the blended solution's pH to 12. The obtained solution was further stirred in dark at 37 °C for 24 h. The reaction was stopped, and the resulting Au NCs solution was filtered and stored at 4 °C.

Synthesis of CDs (Ma et al., 2023): Citric acid anhydrous (1 g) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) (0.5 g) were applied as the carbon source and the nitrogen source, respectively. The solution was dissolved with vigorous stirring, and then heated for 6 h at 200 °C. Further purification was performed using a 1000 Da molecular weight dialysis bag and stored at 4 °C.

Synthesis of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8: The preparation of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 was prepared according to modified literature methods (Y. Shi et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2023; Yang, Jia, Hou, Zhao, & Wang, 2022). 4 mL of the as prepared Au NCs was added in an Eppendorf tube, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 6 with diluted HCl to generate the Au NCs precipitate based on centrifugation. The obtained Au NCs precipitate and 200 μL CDs were added to 100 mL of methanol and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (5 mmol) and 2-methylimidazole (20 mmol) were added and stirred for 24 h to obtain the CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8. After centrifugation, the sample was vacuum-dried for a whole night at 50 °C after three methanol washes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of deep learning integrated sensing on smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Smartphones are a feasible choice for many sensing applications due to their portability, mobility, and high-resolution cameras. Here, deep learning techniques were implanted on a portable smartphone for the independent development of two WeChat APPs entitled “RGB” and “96 Speckles”. The specific parameters are shown in the Supporting Information .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-mode logic gate for tetracycline detection
ce:section.ce:para: Smartphone (FL): Different concentrations of the four tetracycline analogues (TTC, OTC, DC, CTC) were mixed in separate wells with 370 μL of sensing probe (CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8) in the 96-well plate in the self-designed toolbox. The treated well plates were put into the self-developed matching dark box. The target image captured by UV light was inputted into the custom WeChat APP “96 Speckles”, and YOLOv8 algorithm was used to locate the whole plate. YOLOv5 algorithm was used to locate the small wells and extract the eigenvalues, and then the RGB values were determined. For high throughput and real-time target detection, the entire plate can be configured and read by a simple piece of software.

Smartphone (VL): A mixture of incubated CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 with TTC, OTC, DC and CTC were filtered and added to the cuvette, respectively. Pictures were taken in daylight using a smartphone (HUAWEI P30 Pro). The acquired pictures were inputted into the self-developed APP “RGB”, and the YOLO algorithm was used to locate and extract the relevant information by combining the image processing algorithm. The RGB/HSV values were read for analysis.
UV: CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 were measured separately with different solutions of TCs (TTC, OTC, DC, CTC) under UV spectrophotometer. The UV–Vis absorption values at 300–600 nm were recorded.

TEM and SEM were used to study the morphology of the nanomaterials before and after ZIF-8 encapsulation of CDs-Au NCs. The TEM results showed the regular morphology of ZIF-8 (Fig. 1A). When compared to ZIF-8, the crystal size of ZIF-8-wrapped CDs-Au NCs was noticeably larger, with more granular dots in the middle and uneven edges (Fig. 1B). These findings demonstrated that ZIF-8 effectively encapsulated CDs-Au NCs. ZIF-8 had a rhombic dodecahedron shape and a homogeneous morphology, according to the SEM image (Fig. 1C). The crystal size after encapsulation of CDs-Au NCs increased significantly and granular dots appeared on the crystals, indicating the presence of CDs-Au NCs in ZIF-8 (Fig. 1D). To further demonstrate the presence of CDs-Au NCs in ZIF-8, the elemental composition of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 was also analyzed by EDS and SEM-Mapping (Figs. 1E-F). The findings demonstrated that the components including C, N, Zn, and Au were distributed in CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8, offering compelling proof of the effective synthesis of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8.

The crystal phases of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 were characterized by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD). The XRD spectra of Fig. 2A show that there is no significant loss of crystallinity of ZIF-8 after encapsulation of CDs-Au NCs, suggesting that the doping of CDs-Au NCs had no effect on ZIF-8's crystalline structure (Wang, Yin, & Gunasekaran, 2023; H. Yang et al., 2021). Moreover, a consistent result was observed from the FT-IR spectra. ZIF-8 and CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 displayed the similar characteristic absorption peaks in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2B). These results also suggested the successful preparation of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 (Hu et al., 2023). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further used to validate the presence of the typical functional groups and chemical composition on CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8. The XPS full spectrum showed that CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 were mainly composed of C, O, N, Zn and Au elements (Fig. 2C) (Wei et al., 2023). Spectroscopy using XPS Precision The spectra further demonstrated that O, Au, and Zn were present in the composite nanomaterials, indicating that CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 was successfully synthesized (Figs. 2D-F) (Wang, Yin, & Gunasekaran, 2023, et al., Wang, Li, et al., 2023).

To explore the optical properties of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8, measurements were carried out using UV–Vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer. There was no significant absorption from 300 nm to 600 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectra for CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 (Fig. S1A). Under the excitation at 365 nm, CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 showed dual fluorescence emission at 440 nm and 640 nm (Fig. S1B). CDs are the source of the blue fluorescence emission peak at 440 nm while Au NCs are the source of the red fluorescence emission peak at 640 nm. Due to the superior masking effect of red fluorescence, the CDs-Au NCs encapsuled ZIF-8 showed a dominant fluorescence color in red. In the presence of TCs, a novel fluorescence peak appeared at 500 nm, while the peaks at 440 nm and 600 nm showed a responsive decrease in the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1C), suggesting the feasibility of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 as a probe for TCs detection.

To explore the optimal conditions for TCs detection, the pH and time of detection were optimized. The intensity of fluorescence changed at pH 3–12, correspondingly, following the addition of TCs (Fig. S2A). CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 exhibited the maximum fluorescent change to TCs at pH 9–11. To avoid unnecessary interference to the detection, pH 9 was chosen for the subsequent experiments. We next continued to investigate the variation of reaction time. The probe responded quickly to TCs within 30 s and stabilized within 5 min (Fig. S2B). Therefore, the optimal incubation time was set as 5 min for the following investigation. Under the optimal conditions, we next explored the repeatability of the CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8, which is essential for enabling routine and low-cost monitoring. The fluorescence change of the reaction system after the addition of TCs was determined, and the fluorescence change remained stable with an RSD of 0.35% (n = 6) after six repetitions (Fig. S2C). The batch-to-batch repeatability of the manufacturing process of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 was also investigated. Each sensor showed a consistent response with an RSD of 0.46% (n = 6), indicating the excellent repeatability of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 (Fig. S2D).

Considering the interference of other possible co-existing substances or metal ions in complex biological systems, the response of simulated interfering molecules to CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 were tested to explore its selectivity (Figs. S3CD). The simulated interfering substances included small molecules (ascorbic acid, threonine, glycine, cysteine, glutamic acid, histaminic acid, aspartic acid, kanamycin, erythromycin, penicillin, maltose, galactose zithromax, thiamphenicol, and amoxicillin), metal ions (K+, Fe3+, Al3+, Zn2+, Li+) and ions (Cl−, F−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−). After the TCs and interfering substances were added to the CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 system, respectively, the ∆F values of each solution were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The concentration of TCs was 30 μM, and the concentration of interfering substances was 10 times that of TCs. The ∆F value of the TCs containing solution decreased sharply, while no significant change was recorded in the ∆F value of the other interfering substance solutions. The addition of TCs to the mixture of interfering substances and CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 system decreased the ∆F values in all solutions. Therefore, the results showed that CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 had high selectivity and anti-interference ability to TCs.

CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 showed no fluorescence emission at 500 nm. However, the addition of TCs triggered the emergence of a novel green fluorescence peak at 500 nm. Previous research has validated the presence of numerous hydrophobic holes within the three-dimensional structure of BSA. TCs were able to enter the hydrophobic structural cavity of the BSA, thereby restricting their rotation in the cavity (Ding et al., 2022; Q. Sun et al., 2017). Therefore, the fluorescence enhancement from CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 after addition of TCs was attributed to the sensitising effect of BSA on TCs. Moreover, the response of ZIF-8, Zn2+ or 2-methylimidazole to TCs were also investigated. The results suggested that the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced only when ZIF-8 was formed (Fig. 3A). This might be attributed to the special structure of ZIF-8, which allowed the clustering between ZIF-8 and TCs through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding (Yang et al., 2022). In the absence or presence of TCs, there was no significant change in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3B), indicating that there was no chemical reaction between ZIF-8 and TCs. Therefore, the detection mechanism may be the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effects of TCs into the ZIF-8 backbone (Si et al., 2022).

To verify the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, we tested the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and Au NCs before and after the addition of TCs. After the addition of TCs, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were almost unchanged (Fig. 3C), whereas the fluorescence lifetimes of Au NCs were altered (Fig. 3D). The UV absorption spectra emission of four TCs (10 μM) changed significantly after incubation with CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8. To further investigate the reaction mechanism, we tested the UV absorption spectra of the four TCs and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs after incubation (Fig. 3E). The results showed that the UV absorption peaks of the incubated TCs partially overlapped with the excitation and emission peaks of the CDs. The fluorescence quenching at 440 nm may be due to the inner IFE (Xu et al., 2023). In addition, the overlap of the UV absorption peaks of the incubated DC and OTC with the excitation and emission peaks of the CDs was greater than that of the TTC and CTC, which explained the greater quenching of the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm after the addition of DC and OTC than that of the TTC and CTC. In Fig. 3F, the Zeta sites of CDs-Au NPs@ZIF-8 and TCs were + 21.1 mV and − 11.6 mV, respectively. The results indicated the electrostatic adsorption between TCs and CDs-Au NPs@ZIF-8 (Yan, Zhang, & Chen, 2023). Combined with the change in fluorescence lifetime of Au NCs, the fluorescence quenching at 640 nm may be due to dynamic quenching effect (Peltomaa et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and visual sensing of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing concentration of TCs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 gradually decreased at 440 and 640 nm, while a novel fluorescence peak (500 nm) emerged in response to the increasing TCs concentration (Figs. 4A-D). The fluorescence color of the probe shifted from red to yellow due to the noticeable shift in the intensity ratio of the emission bands, suggesting that it was possible to visually recognize TCs under UV light. Based on the above conditions, we constructed a three-emission fluorescence sensor based on CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 and successfully used it for the quantitative detection of trace TCs. The quenching rates of ΔF were linearly correlated with DC, OTC, CTC and TTC concentrations (Figs. 4I-L), and the corresponding linear regression equations were y = −0.13133× + 4.74383 (R2 = 0.9902), y = −0.03494× + 2.3663 (R2 = 0.99637), y = −0.09259× + 4.77441 (R2 = 0.9936), y = −0.04114× + 4.13575 (R2 = 0.99829), y = −0.05993× + 4.08409 (R2 = 0.99504) and y = −0.18729× + 5.12074 (R2 = 0.9938), respectively. The linear ranges are 1–50 μM, 3–45 μM, 1–40 μM and 1–40 μM, respectively. The limits of detection were 0.092 μM, 0.244 μM, 0.204 μM and 0.162 μM, respectively.

Based on the fluorescence spectral data, CIE 1931 chromaticity maps were plotted for DC, OTC, CTC, and TTC DC titrations (Figs. 4E-H). The fluorescence color shifts from a red region to a green region with the addition of DC, OTC, CTC, or TTC, which is congruent with the shift in the visible hue of the fluorescence. Meanwhile, we investigated the interaction between CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 and TCs using 3D fluorescence spectroscopy (Figs. S3AB). CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 alone showed emission peaks at 440 nm and 640 nm; when TCs were added, distinct peaks appeared at 500 nm, while the peaks at 440 and 640 nm diminished. Therefore, the proposed triple-emission CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 fluorescent sensor has good sensitivity for the detection of trace TCs in aqueous solution, and has good visual recognition ability for visual detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical analysis of TCs in milk and river water
ce:section.ce:para: We next estimated the recoveries of the samples using the standard addition method, and established the TC concentration in milk and river water to further validate the viability of TCs detection in real samples (Table S1). The spiked recoveries of DC, OTC, TTC and CTC in milk was 101.3–104.1%, 99.20–106.70%, 101.05–109.30%, and 101.40–106.95%, respectively. The recoveries in river water are 99.85%–101.70%, 101.20%–104.10%, 100.70%–103.50% and 99.75%–104.80%. The results showed that the triple emission sensor has good applicability in the determination of milk and river water samples. The accuracy and reliability of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 for the detection of TCs were verified using HPLC method as a reference (Table S2). The results showed that the recoveries of HPLC were in the range of 96.35%–109.4% and the RSDs were in the range of 1.54%–4.27%. These results indicate that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor can reliably determine TCs in real samples and can be used for the detection of TCs in real environments.

To accomplish high-throughput detection, we developed a new application called “96 Speckles” to identify 96-well plates using the YOLOv5 and YOLOv8 algorithms. In terms of model framework, YOLOv5 used a simple Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture (Fig. S4B). Compared to the YOLOv3 model (Fig. S4A), YOLOv5 had some modifications to the stem and neck to enhance functionality, while YOLOv8 contains more convolutional layers and parameters (Fig. 5B), resulting in higher detection accuracy. The network design of YOLOv8 was more intricate and had more branches and Residual Units. As YOLOv8 had a more complicated model than YOLOv5, it exhibited better performance in terms of accuracy. However, due to its smaller and simpler model, YOLOv5 was faster than YOLOv8 in terms of speed. Detailed structural comparisons are provided in
the supporting information . Therefore, both YOLOv8 and YOLOv5 algorithms were successively applied in the present study to locate the entire 96-well plate for model training.

In this study, the YOLOv8 algorithm was used to identify the outer frame of the aperture plate, while the YOLOv5 algorithm was used to status the inner part of the aperture plate with improved accuracy over YOLOv8 algorithm. The dataset for this 96-well plate-based edge detection training included 720 fluorescence images. A custom-designed dark box was used to shoot all of the photos, which were stored as JPG files with a resolution of 3648 by 2736 pixels (Fig. 5A). The “tag img” tool was used to tag and preserve the 180 photos. 90% of these photos were utilized for training, while the remaining 10% were used for testing. Therefore, the accurate positioning of 96-well plates was achieved by learning from the YOLO series of algorithms.

Effectiveness assessment of YOLOv5 and YOLOv8:
(1)
precision =
TP
TP + FP
(2)
Re call =
TP
TP + FN
(3)
mAP =
1 k
∑
i = 1
k
AP i

True positives (TP) referred to the quantity of accurately recognized images. False positives (FP) referred to the quantity of inaccurate photos that were correctly identified. True negatives (TN) referred to the quantity of false photos that were reliably identified. False negatives (FN) represented the quantity of correct photos that are misidentified. AP and mAP referred to average precision and the mean of all the AP categories, respectively. APi referred to the multiplication of A and Pi. The results showed that the model had good performance in identifying the targets with outstanding accuracy (Table S3).

To achieve the goal of high-throughput rapid screening, a APP entitled “96 Speckles” was developed by our research group using YOLOv5 and YOLOv8 algorithms (Fig. 6 ). The app located the entire 96-well plate and identified small holes in the plate before extracting color features. Compared to the first generation of our cuvette-based applet, this APP was significantly more efficient since it enabled the simultaneous detection of 96 samples for each test. “96 Speckles” involved three functions, namely “Plate”, “Liner” and “classify”.

For the first function “Plate”, the fluorescence image of the 96-well plate was input and the appropriate threshold was selected to recognize the image. After processing, the RGB values of the 96 holes were derived simultaneously. The second function “Liner” was mainly used for linear analysis. After uploading the 96-well plate fluorescence photos, the target wells were manually selected to analyze the linearity and the corresponding concentrations were entered for analysis. YOLOv8 was used to identify the large frame, while YOLOv5 was used to locate each well and extract its RGB value. At the bottom of the page, the ideal color ratio was chosen, and its linear analysis was performed using the concentration of TCs. Finally, the best linear equations and correlation coefficients were obtained for DC, OTC, TTC and CTC as y = 0.0339× + 0.3220, R2 = 0.9931; y = 0.0445× + 0.3444, R2 = 0.9931; y = 0.01620× + 0.4595, R2 = 0.9911; and y = 0.02702× + 0.2542, R2 = 0.989. The linear ranges were 2–30 μM, 1–25 μM, 2–25 μM and 4–30 μM, respectively. The third function “classify”, focused on color classification by entering a clear fluorescent image and selecting a suitable threshold. After recognition, the system classified and named each hole by its color to quickly filter and locate the target hole.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric sensing mechanism of different TCs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7A, the chemical structures of TTC, OTC, CTC, and DC are very similar, thereby leading to the difficulty in their differentiation. CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 alone showed no significant UV absorption peaks at 320–700 nm, while TTC, DC, OTC, and CTC all showed strong absorption values at 360 nm (dashed line, Fig. 7B). We further explored the changes in the UV–Vis absorption peaks of CD- Au NCs@ZIF-8 upon addition of 10 μM of each of the four TCs. OTC and TTC showed a bright red shift, while DC remained unchanged, and CTC showed an obvious blue shift (solid line, Fig. 7B). As shown in Fig. S5, a significant difference in the signal peak of CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 at 550 nm was observed upon increasing TCs concentrations (Fig. 7C). The visibility value at 550 nm for different concentrations of DC and TTC were all greater than 0.2, whereas the UV absorption intensities of OTC and CTC were all less than 0.2 at the highest concentration, further distinguishing TTC, DC, OTC and CTC. TTC and OTC had an extremely high degree of similarity in their functional groups, and therefore show consistency in redshift. The functional groups of CTC and DC (Y. Yang et al., 2019) are significantly different, which may be the main reason for affecting the UV absorption peaks.

As CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 sensor has high selectivity and anti-interference to TCs, we next explored the detection of TCs using deep learning-assisted smartphone. As shown in Fig. S6, the four TCs analogues were analyzed using an applet entitled “96 speckles”. The fluorescent samples in the yellow-green range were marked as “1” and the samples with red fluorescence or other colors were marked as “0”. To achieve the discrimination of TTC, OTC, CTC and DC, we also utilized the property of their UV absorption peaks and used the difference in absorption at 370 nm and 360 nm as a reference (∆X). The values greater than 0 were labelled as “1”, and those less than 0 were labelled as “0”.

To further simplify the operation process, we used another self-developed APP to compare the colors for rapid differentiation of TCs. Specifically, the pictures of five different concentrations of TCs reacted with CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 in daylight were taken. As shown in Figs. 8A-D, the photos were input into the “RGB” app, and each cuvette position was located manually to obtain the RGB and HSV values. CTC showed a yellowish color after incubation with CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8, in which the H (grey value) was always greater than 40 (Figs. 8A). OTC showed almost no obvious color change after incubation with CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8, and the H value was always greater than 40 (Figs. 8B). The color changes of DC, TTC and CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 are shown in Figs. 8C-D, respectively. The deepening of the color was clearly recorded by naked eyes and the H-value of both of them were less than 40. Therefore, we output “0” for those greater than 40 and “1” for those less than 40. The feature values extracted by deep learning were compared with the trend of the visibility value at 550 nm, and the pattern was found to be consistent, further reducing the error. Combining the fluorescence and UV analyses mentioned above, the final output value of “111” corresponded to DC, and “100”, “110” and “101” referred to OTC, CTC, TTC, respectively (Figs. 8E). As the existing rapid sensing methods failed to discriminate each analogue from the four tetracyclines (Table S4), the platform in the present study has superior discriminatory performance in terms of sensitivity, anti-interference ability, and stability and has broad prospects for practical application.

In summary, a novel encapsuled material CDs-Au NCs@ZIF-8 was successfully synthesized for the construction of fluorescence colorimetric multi-mode logic gates based on a deep learning-assisted smartphone. The logic gate enabled the identification of four analogues of tetracycline antibiotics. When TCs were present, a linear response was observed owing to the sensitization interactions with BSA, π-π interactions with ZIF-8, and hydrogen bonding between ZIF-8. The emergence of a novel fluorescence emission peak at 500 nm was triggered with the fluorescence emission shifting from red to yellow color. We used 96-well plates and the self-developed WeChat APP “96 Speckle” to perform a rapid and large number of primary screenings to screen out TCs from the unknown solution. The screened solutions were then subjected to UV analysis and colorimetric analysis. Using the characteristics of different tetracycline structural formulas and colors under daylight, special coding of specific antibiotics was achieved through signal input and output. In turn, TTC, OTC, CTC, and TC were identified step-by-step. The results showed that the platform is practical and offers the possibility of personalized, rapid food safety monitoring.

Ping Tan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yuhui Chen: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Hongrong Chang: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Tao Liu: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation. Jian Wang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhiwei Lu: Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Mengmeng Sun: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Gehong Su: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yanying Wang: Supervision, Project administration. Huimin David Wang: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Chunghang Leung: Supervision, Resources. Hanbing Rao: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chun Wu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the following grants: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2023NSFSC0618), Two-Way Support Team Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (P202105), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (22104102, 22301201 and 32271352), Tianfu Emei Young Science & Technology Talents from Sichuan Province (NO. 2618).
